title: The Repair Café
summary: A narrator describes a community repair café where volunteers fix broken objects brought in by neighbours. The atmosphere is warm and the impact on the local community is surprisingly profound.
body: Every Saturday morning, a small church hall at the end of our street transforms into something quite remarkable. Tables are set up, tools are laid out, and volunteers arrive with their skill sets and their patience. This is our local repair café, and it has become one of my favourite places in the whole neighbourhood. People start arriving shortly after nine o'clock. They carry all sorts of things: a toaster that stopped working, a coat with a broken zip, a child's toy with a missing wheel. Some of them look a little embarrassed, as if bringing a broken object is somehow a sign of failure. But the volunteers — mostly retired engineers, seamstresses, and electricians — greet everyone with a warm smile and immediately get to work. What strikes me most about the repair café is the conversation that takes place while things are being fixed. A man in his seventies was repairing an old radio last month while its owner, a young woman who had inherited it from her grandmother, stood watching. They talked for nearly an hour about music, about memory, and about why some things are worth holding on to. I overheard them laughing together by the end. The volunteers are not paid for their work. They give up their Saturday mornings because they believe that we throw away too much. "Everything has become disposable," one of the seamstresses told me. "But if you take the time to fix something, it lasts. And you feel proud of it." She had just finished repairing a beloved winter jacket that its owner had not been able to wear for three years. The owner held it up and beamed. It is not just objects that get repaired here. Something subtler happens too. Strangers become neighbours. People who might never have spoken discover shared interests. The café has reduced loneliness in our community in a way that no council initiative ever quite managed. Regulars often come back not because they have something broken, but because they enjoy the atmosphere. One elderly man told me he comes every week simply to have someone to talk to. The repair café costs very little to run. It relies on donated tools, a modest hall hire fee, and a great deal of goodwill. Yet its impact far outweighs its budget. In a world that often feels rushed and wasteful, it reminds us that slowing down, taking care, and working together can make a real difference. I always leave feeling better than when I arrived.

annotations.exampleEn: The empty lot transforms into a garden every spring.
The team brought different skill sets to the project.
She arrived shortly after the meeting started.

annotations.meaningVi: dấu hiệu của sự thất bại
annotations.exampleEn: He felt that asking for help was a sign of failure.
Once everyone arrived, we got to work immediately.

annotations.meaningVi: điều ấn tượng tôi nhất, điều khiến tôi chú ý nhất
annotations.exampleEn: What strikes me most is how calm she seems.
The ceremony takes place in the main hall.

annotations.meaningVi: thừa kế từ, được truyền lại từ
annotations.exampleEn: She inherited the watch from her grandfather.

annotations.meaningVi: đáng để giữ lại, đáng trân trọng
annotations.exampleEn: Some friendships are worth holding on to.

annotations.meaningVi: hi sinh, từ bỏ (thời gian, công sức)
annotations.exampleEn: She gave up her weekends to help at the shelter.

annotations.meaningVi: dùng một lần, có thể vứt đi
annotations.exampleEn: We use too many disposable products in daily life.
She beamed with pride when she received the award.

annotations.meaningVi: tinh tế hơn, khó nhận thấy hơn
annotations.exampleEn: There was a subtler change in his attitude over time.
Community events help reduce loneliness among elderly people.

annotations.meaningVi: vượt xa, lớn hơn nhiều so với
annotations.exampleEn: The benefits far outweigh the costs.

annotations.meaningVi: sáng kiến của hội đồng địa phương
annotations.exampleEn: The council initiative aimed to improve public spaces.
The project relied entirely on goodwill and donations.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice — Describing processes and actions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong bài, câu bị động được dùng nhiều để mô tả các hoạt động diễn ra mà không cần nhấn mạnh người thực hiện. Ví dụ: "Tables are set up", "tools are laid out", "things are being fixed". Cấu trúc: be + past participle. Dạng tiếp diễn bị động (are being fixed) nhấn mạnh hành động đang xảy ra tại thời điểm đó.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Passive voice is used extensively here to describe ongoing activities without focusing on who performs them. Structure: be + past participle. The continuous passive (are being fixed) emphasises that the action is in progress at the time described.

grammarNotes.title: Reported Speech — Sharing what others said
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng lời tường thuật để kể lại điều người khác nói. Ví dụ: "one of the seamstresses told me" và "One elderly man told me he comes every week". Khi động từ tường thuật ở quá khứ, động từ trong mệnh đề phụ thường lùi thì — nhưng nếu điều được nói vẫn còn đúng, có thể giữ nguyên thì hiện tại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Reported speech is used to relay what others said. When the reporting verb is past tense ('told me'), the verb in the reported clause typically shifts back in tense. However, if the statement is still true, the present tense can be kept, as in 'told me he comes every week'.

translationVi:
Mỗi sáng thứ Bảy, một hội trường nhà thờ nhỏ ở cuối phố chúng tôi biến đổi thành một nơi khá đặc biệt. Những chiếc bàn được kê ra, dụng cụ được bày lên, và các tình nguyện viên đến với kỹ năng và sự kiên nhẫn của mình. Đây là quán café sửa chữa địa phương của chúng tôi, và nó đã trở thành một trong những nơi tôi yêu thích nhất trong cả khu phố.

Mọi người bắt đầu đến ngay sau chín giờ. Họ mang đến đủ thứ: một cái máy nướng bánh mì hỏng, một chiếc áo khoác đứt dây kéo, một món đồ chơi trẻ em mất bánh xe. Một vài người trông có vẻ hơi ngại ngùng, như thể mang đồ vật hỏng đến là dấu hiệu của sự thất bại. Nhưng các tình nguyện viên — hầu hết là những kỹ sư, thợ may và thợ điện đã về hưu — đón chào mọi người bằng nụ cười ấm áp và ngay lập tức bắt tay vào làm.

Điều ấn tượng tôi nhất về quán café sửa chữa là những cuộc trò chuyện diễn ra trong khi đồ vật đang được sửa. Tháng trước, một người đàn ông ngoài bảy mươi tuổi đang sửa một chiếc radio cũ trong khi chủ nhân của nó, một cô gái trẻ được thừa kế từ bà ngoại, đứng nhìn. Họ đã nói chuyện gần một tiếng về âm nhạc, về ký ức, và về lý do tại sao một số thứ đáng để giữ lại. Tôi vô tình nghe thấy họ cùng cười vào cuối buổi.

Các tình nguyện viên không được trả tiền cho công việc của họ. Họ dành buổi sáng thứ Bảy vì họ tin rằng chúng ta vứt bỏ quá nhiều. "Mọi thứ đã trở nên dùng một lần rồi vứt," một trong những người thợ may nói với tôi. "Nhưng nếu bạn dành thời gian để sửa chữa thứ gì đó, nó sẽ bền lâu. Và bạn sẽ cảm thấy tự hào về nó." Bà vừa hoàn thành việc sửa một chiếc áo khoác mùa đông mà chủ nhân của nó đã không thể mặc trong ba năm. Người chủ giơ áo lên và rạng rỡ mỉm cười.

Không chỉ có đồ vật được sửa chữa ở đây. Điều gì đó tinh tế hơn cũng xảy ra. Người lạ trở thành hàng xóm. Những người có thể chưa bao giờ nói chuyện với nhau lại khám phá ra sở thích chung. Quán café đã giảm bớt sự cô đơn trong cộng đồng theo cách mà không một sáng kiến nào của hội đồng địa phương có thể làm được. Những khách hàng quen thường quay lại không phải vì họ có đồ hỏng, mà vì họ thích bầu không khí ở đây. Một cụ ông nói với tôi rằng ông đến mỗi tuần chỉ để có người trò chuyện.

Quán café sửa chữa tốn rất ít chi phí để vận hành. Nó dựa vào dụng cụ được tặng, một khoản phí thuê hội trường khiêm tốn, và rất nhiều thiện chí. Tuy nhiên, tác động của nó vượt xa ngân sách. Trong một thế giới thường cảm thấy vội vã và lãng phí, nó nhắc nhở chúng ta rằng chậm lại, chăm sóc nhau, và cùng nhau làm việc có thể tạo ra sự khác biệt thực sự. Tôi luôn ra về với tâm trạng tốt hơn khi đến.

questions.prompt: Where does the repair café take place?
options: In a community centre near the market
In a small church hall at the end of the narrator's street
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'a small church hall at the end of our street transforms into something quite remarkable.'
questions.hint: Think about what building hosts the repair café.

questions.prompt: When does the repair café open?
options: Every Saturday morning, shortly after nine o'clock
questions.explanation: The narrator states people 'start arriving shortly after nine o'clock' every Saturday morning.
questions.hint: The narrator mentions the day and approximate time.

questions.prompt: Why do some visitors look embarrassed when they arrive?
options: They do not know how to describe their broken objects
They feel that bringing a broken object suggests they have failed
They are afraid the volunteers will charge too much
They have never visited the café before
questions.explanation: The narrator says some people 'look a little embarrassed, as if bringing a broken object is somehow a sign of failure.'
questions.hint: Consider how the narrator describes the visitors' feelings.

questions.prompt: What were the elderly man and the young woman talking about while the radio was being repaired?
options: Music, memory, and why some things are worth keeping
questions.explanation: They talked 'about music, about memory, and about why some things are worth holding on to.'
questions.hint: Think about what objects like old radios make people remember.

questions.prompt: Why do the volunteers give up their Saturday mornings?
options: They are paid a small wage by the council
They want to learn new skills
They believe we throw away too much and that fixing things is worthwhile
They are required to volunteer as part of their retirement contract
questions.explanation: The narrator explains volunteers 'give up their Saturday mornings because they believe that we throw away too much.'
questions.hint: The seamstress gives a clue about their philosophy.

questions.prompt: What does the seamstress mean when she says 'Everything has become disposable'?
options: Modern products are made to be repaired easily
People throw things away instead of repairing them
Volunteers should be paid for their work
Old clothes are more fashionable than new ones
questions.explanation: She is saying that society treats everything as something to be thrown away after use, rather than repaired and kept.
questions.hint: 'Disposable' means something designed to be used once and thrown away.

questions.prompt: According to the narrator, what is the subtler benefit of the repair café beyond fixing objects?
options: It teaches people to repair their own homes
It helps strangers become neighbours and reduces loneliness
It raises money for local charities
It trains young people in traditional crafts
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Strangers become neighbours' and the café 'has reduced loneliness in our community.'
questions.hint: Think about the social effects mentioned.

questions.prompt: Why does the elderly man come to the repair café every week?
options: He always has something to be repaired
He enjoys the atmosphere and wants someone to talk to
He is learning to fix radios
questions.explanation: He 'comes every week simply to have someone to talk to.'
questions.hint: His reason is not about objects at all.

questions.prompt: How does the repair café manage its costs?
options: It receives a large government grant
It charges visitors a fixed fee per repair
It relies on donated tools, a modest hall hire fee, and goodwill
It is funded entirely by corporate sponsors
questions.explanation: The narrator says it 'relies on donated tools, a modest hall hire fee, and a great deal of goodwill.'
questions.hint: Look for the sentence where the narrator explains how the café is funded.

questions.prompt: What feeling does the narrator describe after leaving the repair café?
questions.explanation: The narrator ends by saying 'I always leave feeling better than when I arrived.'
questions.hint: This is the final sentence of the passage.

Tables are set up, tools are laid out, and volunteers arrive with their skill sets and their patience. This is our local repair café, and it has become one of my favourite places in the whole neighbourhood. People start arriving shortly after nine o'clock. They carry all sorts of things: a toaster that stopped working, a coat with a broken zip, a child's toy with a missing wheel. Some of them look a little {{b2}}, as if bringing a broken object is somehow a sign of failure. But the volunteers — mostly retired engineers, seamstresses, and electricians — greet everyone with a warm smile and immediately get to work. What {{b3}} me most about the repair café is the conversation that takes place while things are being fixed. A man in his seventies was repairing an old radio last month while its owner, a young woman who had {{b4}} it from her grandmother, stood watching. They talked for nearly an hour about music, about memory, and about why some things are worth holding on to. I overheard them laughing together by the end. The volunteers are not paid for their work. They give up their Saturday mornings because they believe that we throw away too much. "Everything has become {{b5}}," one of the seamstresses told me. "But if you take the time to fix something, it lasts. And you feel proud of it." She had just finished repairing a beloved winter jacket that its owner had not been able to wear for three years. The owner held it up and {{b6}}. It is not just objects that get repaired here. Something {{b7}} happens too. Strangers become neighbours. People who might never have spoken discover shared interests. The café has {{b8}} loneliness in our community in a way that no council initiative ever quite managed. Regulars often come back not because they have something broken, but because they enjoy the atmosphere. One elderly man told me he comes every week simply to have someone to talk to. The repair café costs very little to run. It relies on donated tools, a modest hall hire fee, and a great deal of {{b9}}. Yet its impact far {{b10}} its budget. In a world that often feels rushed and wasteful, it reminds us that slowing down, taking care, and working together can make a real difference. I always leave feeling better than when I arrived.

blanks.explanation: 'Transforms into' is the exact phrasal verb used in the passage, meaning to change completely into something different.
The passage says some visitors 'look a little embarrassed' because they feel bringing a broken object is a sign of failure.
'What strikes me most' is an idiomatic expression meaning 'what impresses or stands out to me the most'.
The young woman 'inherited' the radio from her grandmother, meaning she received it after her grandmother died.
The seamstress says 'Everything has become disposable,' meaning people treat objects as things to throw away rather than repair.
'Beamed' means to smile very brightly and happily, conveying the owner's joy at having her jacket repaired.
'Subtler' means more difficult to notice — the social benefits are less obvious than the physical repairs.
The café has 'reduced loneliness,' meaning it has made the community less lonely, though not eliminated loneliness entirely.
'Goodwill' refers to the friendly, helpful attitude of people who contribute time and resources without expecting payment.
'Far outweighs' means the impact is much greater than the cost — a collocation used to compare two things of very different sizes.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator suggests the repair café fixes more than just broken objects — it repairs social bonds too. Do you think the decline of repair culture is connected to a broader decline in community? What would it take to bring that kind of culture back in a modern city?
